In Ezekiel 46, we encounter a vision of restored temple worship where every detail—from the prince's entry to the people's movement, from daily offerings to the rhythms of Sabbaths and New Moons—speaks to something we easily forget: worship is never casual. The Lord God specifies which gates open when, how many unblemished animals must be offered, the precise measurements of grain and oil, and even the direction worshipers must walk. This is not the work of a God indifferent to how His people approach Him. It is the work of a God who sees significance in order, consistency, and deliberate movement toward His presence. When we read these instructions, we must resist the urge to dismiss them as merely ceremonial. They reveal that approaching God requires intention, preparation, and a willingness to follow His design rather than our own preferences.

What strikes us most forcefully is the principle introduced in verse 16-18: the prince, despite his position, cannot simply take what he wants. His inheritance passes to his sons, not scattered among those who serve him temporarily. More crucially, he must never displace the Lord's people from their property. Here is power under constraint. Here is a leader whose position grants Him authority not to accumulate but to steward, not to grasp but to guard the people's security. This is a radical reframing of leadership that stands against every instinct of fallen human nature. The prince's freedom is bound by justice toward those beneath him. His wealth comes with limits. And if we hear in this the foreshadowing of the Messiah—the ultimate Prince who would not exploit but empty Himself, who would not take but give—we begin to sense how deeply the vision penetrates the corruption of human rule.

The closing verses return us to the practical reality of worship: the kitchens in the outer court where the sacrifices are cooked, the careful separation of the preparation areas so that ordinary people are not overwhelmed by the weight of holiness transmitted prematurely. Even in restoration, there is the acknowledgment that we need mediation, gradual approach, preparation. The vision does not flatten the distance between the holy and the common—it bridges it carefully. For those of us who live this side of Christ's perfect priesthood, we must ask what this teaches us about our own approach to God. Are we approaching Him with the deliberateness this chapter commands? Or have we made worship so casual, so accommodating to our comfort, that we have forgotten we are entering the presence of the living God? The chapter closes without fanfare, simply showing us how God's people would eat, serve, and sustain the sacrifice. This too is worship—the faithful, daily work of maintaining what God has established.
